title: Gainsborough: The Fashion of Portraiture
content:
The English artist Thomas Gainsborough (1727–1788) is widely celebrated as an independent and original genius whose true passion was painting landscapes. However, portraits were the most popular form of painting during his time, and they were necessary to pay the rent. Gainsborough famously referred to this lucrative but tedious work as the "curs'd face business." The hours were long, and he often found his wealthy clients, especially gentlemen, to be quite burdensome. He even quipped that these gentlemen had only one part worth looking at: their purse.

Beyond dealing with difficult patrons, Gainsborough faced the tricky artistic challenge of selecting the right clothing for his portraits. He had to decide what attire would transcend current fashion and stand the test of time. Furthermore, he had to determine what creative liberties he could take with the dresses and suits themselves. While most portraitists hired specialists to paint complex fabrics like flashing taffetas and melton wools, Gainsborough employed no drapery painter. He mastered the rendering of delicate fabrics entirely without external assistance.

This distinct, independent approach is beautifully highlighted in modern exhibitions focusing on his portraiture. Viewers are drawn to the exquisite 18th-century ribbons and laces, eager to see the vivid pigments used to achieve his signature hues. Exhibitions showcasing his work perfectly capture his ability to bring garments to life using rich colors like indigo and ultramarine. His imperious portraits remain a testament to his remarkable talent and dedication. Ultimately, Gainsborough elevated the "curs'd face business" into timeless masterpieces that continue to captivate audiences.
